coredata.dc:title: Antitumor/antiviral carbon quantum dots based on carrageenan and pullulan
coredata.dc:creator: Emam, Hossam E.
Ahmed, Hanan B.
coredata.dc:description: Requirement for medication from pathogenic human viruses and cancer diseases are urgently considered, while, numerous reports were focused on investigating easily manufactured and excellently effective therapeutic reagents. Herein, CQDs were prepared with size of 2.1 nm from both of carrageenan and pullulan. CQDs nucleated from pullulan showed higher anti-proliferative effects against cancer cells, while, treatment with 100 μg/mL of CQDs colloids originated from pullulan and carrageenan separately resulted in diminishing of cancer cell viability percent to be 42.1 & 58.7%, respectively. Plaque reduction assay was also affirmed that, 2.5 μg/L of both of pullulan and carrageenan based CQDs exhibited viral inhibition with percent of 44.3& 59.5%, respectively. As a conclusion, pullulan showed seniority over carrageenan in nucleation of CQDs with higher anticancer activities. While, estimation of antiviral performance of the prepared CQDs confirmed the priority of carrageenan compared to pullulan in preparation of CQDs as antiviral laborer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrophilic CQDs
Carrageenan
Pullulan
Antitumor
Anti-MERS-CoV

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell proliferation assay and anticancer potency
Mechanism for synthesis of hydrophilic CQDs
Anticancer potentiality of CQDs for apoptotic cell death

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for synthesis of hydrophilic CQDs via exploitation of carrageenan and pullulan separately.
Photographic images and absorbance spectra for the biopolymers and the prepared CQDs before and after dialysis, [a] carrageenan and [b] pullulan.

Transition electron micrographs for CQDs before and after dialysis prepared under 10 h hydrothermal time; [a, b] carrageenan, [c, d] pullulan, [a, c] before dialysis and [b, d] after dialysis. [e] Particle size from zetasizer analyzer.
[a, b] Raman spectra for CQDs before and after dialysis; [a] carrageenan and [b] pullulan. [c, d] FTIR spectra for CQDs after dialysis prepared from; [c] carrageenan and [d] pullulan.
NMR spectra for CQDs after dialysis prepared from; [a, c] carrageenan, [b, d] pullulan, [a, b] 1H NMR and [c, d] 13C NMR.
Fluorescence spectra (FL) for CQDs before and after dialysis prepared from; [a] carrageenan and [b] pullulan.

Anticancer activity for the prepared CQDs against breast cancer cells; [a] images of measured kits, [b] optical density, [c] number of died cells, [d] cell viability percentage and [e] schematic diagram for anticancer potency.
Antiviral activity for the prepared CQDs against coronavirus (MERS-CoV); [a] viral viability, [b] viral inhibition percentage and [c] schematic diagram for antiviral performance.

head.ce:title: Antitumor/antiviral carbon quantum dots based on carrageenan and pullulan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pretreatment and Finishing of Cellulosic based Textiles, Textile Industries Research Division, National Research Centre, Scopus affiliation ID 60014618, 33 EL Buhouth St., Dokki, Giza 12622, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pretreatment and Finishing of Cellulosic based Textiles
National Research Centre, Scopus affiliation ID 60014618

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pretreatment and Finishing of Cellulosic based Textiles, Textile Industries Research Division, National Research Centre, Scopus affiliation ID 60014618, 33 EL Buhouth St., Dokki, Giza 12622, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Helwan University, Ain-Helwan, Cairo 11795, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Helwan University, Ain-Helwan, Cairo 11795, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Requirement for medication from pathogenic human viruses and cancer diseases are urgently considered, while, numerous reports were focused on investigating easily manufactured and excellently effective therapeutic reagents. Herein, CQDs were prepared with size of 2.1 nm from both of carrageenan and pullulan. CQDs nucleated from pullulan showed higher anti-proliferative effects against cancer cells, while, treatment with 100 μg/mL of CQDs colloids originated from pullulan and carrageenan separately resulted in diminishing of cancer cell viability percent to be 42.1 & 58.7%, respectively. Plaque reduction assay was also affirmed that, 2.5 μg/L of both of pullulan and carrageenan based CQDs exhibited viral inhibition with percent of 44.3& 59.5%, respectively. As a conclusion, pullulan showed seniority over carrageenan in nucleation of CQDs with higher anticancer activities. While, estimation of antiviral performance of the prepared CQDs confirmed the priority of carrageenan compared to pullulan in preparation of CQDs as antiviral laborer.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were uniquely discovered as a new class of carbon nanostructures [1], with particle size ≤10 nm size, while, graphene nano-sheets with a plane size lesser than 100 nm were recognized as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [2]. CQDs were firstly investigated in 2004 during the purification process of single-walled carbon nanotubes [3], where, Xu et al. were known as the first researching group who discovered CQDs in 2004 and taken it into account as a new type of nano-carbonaceous structures with photoluminescence properties [4,5].

Afterwards, investigation of numerous synthetic methods for CQDs was an intensive researching field, owing to their wide scaled applicability in extraordinary fields of photovoltaic devices, optoelectronics, bio-sensing, photocatalysis, drug delivery, and bio-imaging [1–3,6]. By comparing with traditional semiconductor quantum dots, the unique properties of CQDs are their biocompatibility, chemical inertness, low toxicity, cost effective, and have the similar fluorescence activities of semiconductor quantum dots [7]. CQDs were ascribed in terms of a surface-functionalized carbogenic core, while, CQDs are quasi-circular carbon nanostructures, composed of amorphous or nanocrystalline centers with graphitic carbon (sp2 carbon) or graphene and graphene-oxide sheets blended via diamond-like sp3 hybridized carbon additions [1,6,7]. Simultaneously, CQDs are mostly terminated with heteroatoms containing moieties on their surface, that facilitate solubility of CQDs in different aqueous solutions and enhancing the potential for additional functionalization compared to the other types of carbon based nanostructures. Attributing to the previously mentioned specific construction and properties, CQDs are advantageous by specific characteristics like interchangeable photoluminescence (PL), distinctive photo-induced electron transfer, effective absorbance of photovoltaic light, and infrared-responsive up-converted photoluminescence [1,6,7].

Sun et al., were the first researchers who used the description of “carbon dot” for photo-luminescent nanosized carbon particles [8], and they illustrated that the radiative recombination of excitons from the surface energy traps that returns to emissive because of surface passivation which is the main reason for the photoluminescence of these nanoparticles. Another report [9] ascribed CQDs to be consisted of carbon core and decorative functional groups on the edge that constructed from hybridization of the chemical groups, while, the decorated surface is defined as photo-luminescent synergistic centers of CQDs. Nitrogen-based CQDs were prepared from citric acid/urea and characterized by blue and green emission by controlling the mass ratio of the precursors [10–14]. Changing the molar ratio of urea and sodium citrate from 3:1 to 6:1, 9:1, and 12:1 leaded to generation of CQDs with emission peaks at 450, 467, 536, and 526 nm, respectively [11].

Shamsipur et al. [15], prepared CQDs with large size distribution from amino acids as nitrogen based molecules. The construction of larger sp2 conjugation for carbons in the cores of prepared CQDs resulted in emission at longer wavelengths with yellow color. While, blue emitted CQDs prepared from basic amino acids and characterized by the smallest size distribution. Owing to their higher content of nitrogen atoms, the CQDs produced by neutral aliphatic and basic amino acids emitted green color. An unexpected blue emitted CQDs synthesized by aromatic amino acids attributing to the original sp2 carbons in the starting materials [15]. Inter-conjugation of the main skeleton for CQDs with heteroatom such as oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur gives variety to the inter-composition of CQDs. In addition to, it might interchange the conduction/valence band position and confer unexpected functions. Doping with nitrogen, non-metallic elements (e.g. Si, B, S and P) and metals (e.g. Mn, Mg, Cu, Zn, Tb, and Ru) are applied for enhancing the quantum yield of CQDs, controlling the emission of CQDs or motivate new functions and applicability [11,12,14,15].

Various reports were extensively considered with investigation of new synthetic methods for preparation of size and shape regulated CQDs, while, ultrasonic treatment [16], electrochemical oxidation [17], hydrothermal treatment [18,72], and laser irradiation [8] were the most common methodologies, while, some of these methodologies have some disadvantages such like, complexity, expensive materials and equipment and low quantum yields. Therefore, requirement of costless methods for synthesis of CQDs with high quantum yield for large scaled application is still an important issue. Hydrothermal method is one of the appropriate synthetic approaches for preparation of carbon quantum dots that can produce highly size regulated CQDs from various resources of organic molecules and carbohydrates [19].

Nucleation of CQDs via hydrothermal technique which was hypothesized to be proceeded via ionization, condensation, polymerization and aromatization, while, the as-produced CQDs were supposed to contain number of mobile electrons and were limited in size range of 1–10 nm to exhibit excellent UV–visible absorption of radiations and non-blinking photoluminescence [20–28], making the prepared CQDs highly applicable for coating biomaterials, nano vectors, nano probes, for bio-related applications [29–33].

Nontoxicity and biocompatibility of CQDs as prerequisite properties for their application in various biomedicine make it uniquely investigated for their exploitation in cancer treatment. Some of researching reports had considerably studied the bio-toxicity of CQDs on different cancer cell lines such as Hep-2 [34], HeLa [35] and A549 [36,37]. Additionally, it was approved that, some of CQDs prepared by using green carbon resources exhibited by both fluorescent characters and bioactivities such as antioxidant, anticancer and anti-inflammatory effects [38–42]. On the other hand, few reported studies were considered with investigation of the antiviral performance of CQDs. While, Dong et al. studied the effect of direct treatment with 5 μg/mL of EDA-CQDs or EPA-CQD for human noroviruses (virus-like-particles; VLPs), GI.1 and GII.4 VLPs, and they found that, CQDs were highly exhibited by effective antiviral potency via inhibition of VLPs binding to histo-blood group antigens (HBGA) receptors on human cells [43]. More recently, Huang, et al. were reported that, CQDs generated from benzoxazine monomer could in-vitro inhibit the infection of life-threatening flaviviruses, porcine parvovirus and adenovirus-associated virus, and it was supposed to be via direct binding to the surface of the virion and impeding the first step of virus-cell contacting [44].

It is a fascinating challenge to prepare CQDs having both of anticancer and antiviral bioactivities via green hydrothermal synthetic technique, to apply as antiviral and antitumor laborers. The current study focused on studying the efficiency of pullulan versus carrageenan as hydroxyl containing and hydroxyl/sulfate containing biopolymers, respectively, to highlight the effect of functional groups in the polymer building units on the decoration and in turn the activity of the nucleated CQDs. According to our knowledge, no reports were considered with presenting of comparative study for monitoring of the effect of biopolymers functional groups variation on the anticancer and antiviral capability of the so-nucleated CQDs. Additionally, preparation of CQDs exhibited by hydrophilicity character is extensively concerned needing less for any of toxic organic solvents, to be biocompatible for application in biomedical purposes. Therefore in the current study, a comparative study was performed between the affinity of carrageenan versus pullulan in nucleation of CQDs as hydrophilic antitumor reagent with cytotoxic effects against both of breast cancer cells and viral cells. The synthesized CQDs were dialyzed for production of monodispersed/highly purified CQDs. Successive synthesis of CQDs by exploitation of carrageenan and pullulan individually was approved via several instrumental analyses like, UV–visible spectroscopy, zetasizer, transmission electron microscopy, Raman spectroscopy, FTIR, 1HNMR and 13CNMR. Consequently, the photoluminescence of the prepared CQDs was monitored. The anticancer potency of the prepared CQDs was examined via WST-1 cell proliferation assay for following up the cell mortality effects against breast cancer cells for carrageenan based CQDs versus pullulan based CQDs. Additionally, the antiviral activity of the prepared CQDs was estimated via plaque reduction assay, while, MTT cytotoxicity assay (TC50) was also performed in order for calculation of cytotoxicity percent.

Carrageenan and pullulan were obtained from El-Nasser Company for Pharmaceuticals and Chemicals – Egypt. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99%) was supported by Merck, Darmstadt–Germany. All chemicals were applicable as received and aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water.

40 g/L of NaOH was used for hydrolysis of 10 g/L biopolymer (carrageenan or pullulan) and subsequently the reaction liquor was left under magnetic stirring for 2 h at 90 °C. Afterwards, in hydrothermal technique for complete nucleation of CQDs, the reaction liquor was transferred to vertical hydrothermal autoclave reactor and then left in oven at 210 °C for 6 h. Finally, the reaction liquor with dark brown color was kept to be cooled at room temperature then dialyzed with distilled water for ultrafiltration to obtain highly purified/monodispersed/pure CQDs, ready for further analyses.

Absorption spectra for biopolymers (carrageenan or pullulan) and the produced CQDs were manifested in the wavelength range of 250–750 nm using a spectrophotometer (Cary 100 UV-VIS, UV–Vis-NIR Systems, from Agilent). Geometrical features and size distribution of the synthesized CQDs were estimated by anticipating of High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope from Japan (JEOL-JEM-1200). The size distribution of CQDs was evaluated by 4 pi analysis software (from USA) for 50 particles at least. The mean size and poly-dispersity for CQDs were also measured by using zetasizer analyzer (Malvern Nano ZS, from Malvern Instruments Ltd – UK) via dynamic light scattering technique at 25 °C in insulated chamber. Raman spectra for the synthesized CQDs before and after dialysis were also detected by using alpha300R – confocal Raman imaging (WITec from focus innovations – Germany). The spectral data were analyzed with diffraction-limited spatial resolution in the range of 500–3500 cm−1.

Infrared spectral analysis was performed by using Jasco FT/IR 6100 spectrometer. The absorbance spectra were detected in range of 500–4000 cm−1 using 15 points smoothing, 4 cm−1 resolution, 64 scanning times with scanning rate of 2 mm·s−1. The spectra of nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR and 13C NMR) were recorded on a Jeol-Ex-300 NMR spectrometer (JEOL – Japan). Photoluminescence spectra for CQDs in ultraviolet-visible spectral range were detected by spectro-fluorometer (JASCO FP8300). The measurements were performed at room temperature with excitation at 340 nm, while, emission was measured at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell proliferation assay and anticancer potency
ce:section.ce:para: Comparative study between the affinity of pullulan and carrageenan as two different types of polysaccharides in nucleation of cytotoxic/antitumor CQDs with mortal effects on breast cancer cells was tested via WST-1 assay. MDA-MB-231: Breast cancer cell line was obtained from Nawah Scientific Inc. (Mokatam, Cairo, Egypt). Cells were maintained in DMEM media supplemented with 100 mg/ml of streptomycin, 100 units/ml of penicillin and 10% of heat-inactivated fetal bovine in humidified, 5% (v/v) CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C. Cell viability was estimated in WST-1 assay using Abcam® kit (ab155902WST-1Cell Proliferation Reagent). Aliquots of 50 μl cell suspension (3 × 103 cells) were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated in complete media for 24 h. Cells were treated with another aliquot of 50 μL media containing CQDs at serial concentrations (10 μg/ml, 100 μg/ml). After 48 h of exposure to CQDs, cells were treated with 10 μL WST-1 reagent and the absorbance was measured after 1 h at 450 nm using a BMG LABTECH®-FLUOstar Omega microplate reader (Allmendgrün, Ortenberg) [45,46].

Antiviral performance of CQDs nucleated from carrageenan versus CQDs originated from pullulan was studied via MMT cytotoxicity assay for evaluation of the concentration of CQDs which was required for killing 50% of viral cells (CC50). In such procedure, samples were diluted with Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM). Stock solutions of the tested CQDs were prepared in 10% DMSO in diluted H2O. The cytotoxic activity was tested in Vero E6 cells by using 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide [47], with mirror modification. Briefly, the cells were seeded in 96 well-plates (100 μl/well at a density of 3×105 cells/ml) and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in 5% CO2. Afterword, cells were treated with various concentrations of CQDs in triplicates. Then after further 24 h, the supernatant was discarded and cells monolayers were washed with sterile phosphate buffer saline (PBS) 3 times and MTT solution (20 μl of 5 mg/ml stock solution) was added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h followed by medium aspiration. In each well, the formed formazan crystals were dissolved with 200 μl of acidified isopropanol (0.04 M HCl in absolute isopropanol = 0.073 ml HCl in 50 ml isopropanol). Absorbance of formazan solutions was measured at 540 nm with 620 nm as a reference wavelength using a multi-well plate reader and the percentage of cytotoxicity compared to the untreated cells was then estimated.

Plaque reduction assay was also carried out for monitoring the antiviral performance of CQDs prepared from pullulan versus that originated from carrageenan according to the method of Hayden et al. [48]. In a six well plate, where, Vero E6 cells (105 cells/ml) were cultivated for 24 h at 37 °C. Middle East respiratory syndrome-related coronavirus isolate NRCE-HKU270 (accession number: KJ477103.2) virus was diluted to give 103 PFU/well and mixed with safe concentration of CQDs colloids and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C before being added to the cells. Growth medium was removed from the cell culture plates and the cells were inoculated with (100 μl/well) virus with CQDs colloids. After 1 h contact time for virus adsorption, 3 ml of DMEM supplemented with 2% agarose and CQDs colloids were added onto the cell monolayer, plates were left to solidify and incubated at 37 °C till formation of viral plaques (3 to 4 days). Formalin (10%) was added for 2 h then plates were stained with 0.1% crystal violet in distilled water. Control wells were included where untreated virus was incubated with Vero E6 cells and finally plaques were counted. Finally, the percentage reduction in plaques formation in comparison to control wells was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for synthesis of hydrophilic CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Comparative study between the affinities of carrageenan contra pullulan for nucleation of hydrophilic CQDs was successively performed via quite simple technique under hydrothermal conditions at alkaline pH as schematically represented in Fig. 1 . The mechanism of CQDs nucleation from alkali dissolved biopolymers could be postulated as follows: firstly the bio-macromolecules are hypothesized to be destructed and fragmented under the effect of strong alkali. Afterwards, under hydrothermal condition, re-polymerization, aromatization and subsequent oxidation, while, size and shape variable carbon nanostructures were ingrained and clustered [49]. Lastly, dialysis was carried out for ultrafiltration to give mono-dispersed/highly purified CQDs with controllable and regulated topographical features [49].

Carrageenan is supposed to be depolymerized under alkaline conditions to its main repeating units of galactose 4-sulfate and 6-anhydro-galactose residues, while, pullulan is alkali-fragmented into its original repeating units of maltotriose. In both cases furfural intermediates are hypothesized to be liberated under hydrothermal conditions [50,51], which in turn is suggested to be polymerized and aromatized under hydrothermal conditions to aromatic oxygen & sulfur containing polymers in case of carrageenan based CQDs, however, for pullulan based CQDs, oxygen containing aromatic polymer is supposed to be produced [52,53].

UV–visible spectral data for alkali hydrolyzed carrageenan and CQDs prepared via exploitation of carrageenan before and after dialysis were plotted in Fig. 2a. While Fig. 2b, showed the spectra of alkali hydrolyzed pullulan compared to CQDs prepared by pullulan before and after dialysis. From the plotted figures, visual observation for the solutions of carrageenan, CQDs before and after dialysis showed that, the turbid yellowish color of alkali hydrolyzed carrageenan was turned to brownish color after exposure to hydrothermal conditions, while after dialysis the color of solution turned to pale yellow. However, in case of pullulan, its turbid colorless solution was turned to yellow color after it functionalization in synthesis of CQDs, and after dialysis the solution became pale yellow in color.

Spectral mapping data of carrageenan based CQDs and pullulan based CQDs showed two absorption bands for CQDs before dialysis at 260–268 nm and 298–302 nm with long tail in visible region, while, the absorption peak at 260–268 nm is characteristic for n-π* transition and the absorbance band detected at 298–302 nm is referring to π-π* transition and electron radiation relaxation [54,55]. This data is in agreement with literature [56], in which the optical absorption spectra of CQDs is basically in ultraviolet range, additionally, it could preliminary affirm that, the prepared CQDs could contain aromatic conjugated nature. However, after dialysis one absorbance band was observed at 265 nm in case of carrageenan based CQDs, while in case of pullulan based CQDs the as-mentioned characteristic peaks of CQDs became less intense due to removal of undesirable carbon nanostructures in dialysis processing to give highly purified/mono-dispersed CQDs.

Fig. 3 represented TEM micrographs of CQDs prepared via exploitation of carrageenan and pullulan biopolymers before and after dialysis, and showed the geometrical shape and topographical features of the synthesized CQDs, while the size distribution was also estimated. The micrographs strongly confirmed the compatibility and the superiority for both of carrageenan and pullulan in synthesis of size and shape regulated CQDs, in addition to affirming the vital role of dialysis processing in purification for obtaining spherical/highly monodispersed CQDs with mean size of 2.1 nm. However, by comparing between TEM image in Fig. 3b form carrageenan based CQDs and that of pullulan based CQDs in Fig. 3d, it could be obviously observed that, carrageenan is much successively exploited in generation of highly dispersed/homogeneous CQDs rather than pullulan. Additionally, zetasizer data further approved TEM observations for successive clustering of small sized and controllable CQDs [57,58]. Comparative study for the particle size measurements detected via DLS analyzer for carrageenan based CQDs and pullulan based CQDs were figured out in Fig. 3e, and the data signed for the more favorability of carrageenan rather than pullulan, while the particle size of CQDs based carrageenan was 3.1 nm to be smaller than that detected for pullulan based CQDs (4.2 nm). It could be attributed to the sulfur and oxygen containing decorative groups which capped CQDs prepared from carrageenan, while in case of pullulan based CQDs the surface decorative groups are mainly assumed to be only oxygen containing groups, and as it is well known that, sulfur exhibited lower electronegativity than oxygen, so sulfur containing functional groups were hypothesized to be superiorly functionalized in conjugation and regulation of smaller sized/homogenously/well dispersed CQDs. So it could be summarized that, the plotted data in Fig. 3 approved the compatibility of both alkali hydrolyzed carrageenan and pullulan in synthesis of hydrophilic CQDs under hydrothermal conditions, however, carrageenan is more favorable to generate highly dispersed/homogenous and smaller size regulated CQDs.

Raman spectral analysis is a type of vibrational molecular spectroscopy which is mainly performed for investigation of the interchanging in the molecular structures of both of carrageenan and pullulan after their exploitation in synthesis of CQDs. Fig. 4a showed Raman spectral data for carrageenan based CQDs before and after dialysis, while, before dialysis there were two characteristic bands at Raman shift of 2140 cm−1 and 3185 cm−1 were detected for C=C and =(C-H), respectively, while, after dialysis, such bands were disappeared and two new bands at 540 cm−1 & 1116 cm−1 characterized for S-S & C=S were observed. However, in Fig. 4b, Raman spectral data of CQDs based pullulan before and after dialysis showed that, regardless to dialysis processing effect, there was only one band at 1104 cm−1 characterized for asymmetric C-O-C. Raman spectral data could be illustrated due to carbonization, cyclization and aromatization for nucleation of CQDs.

Fig. 4c & d represents FT-IR spectral data for CQDs prepared from alkali hydrolyzed carrageenan and pullulan under hydrothermal conditions. Fig. 5a showed the characteristic peaks of native carrageenan, while, the absorption bands observed at 3320 cm−1, 2944 cm−1, 1609 cm−1, 1418 cm−1, 1210 cm−1, 1022 cm−1 904 cm−1 and 855 cm−1 were characteristic for O-H stretching, interlayer C-H stretching, polymer bond with water, sulfate stretch, ester sulfate group C=O, glycosidic linkage, 3,6-anhydro-D-galactose, and CO-S axial secondary sulfate on C-4 of galactose, respectively. However, all the detected bands were retained after exploitation of carrageenan in nucleation of CQDs [59,60]. On the other hand, Fig.5b displayed the characteristic bands of native pullulan were also retained after its functionalization in synthesis CQDs, while the absorption bands detected at 3300 cm−1 is due to O-H stretching vibrations and that at 2908 cm−1 is referring to the sp3 C–H bond. The absorption peak at 1620 cm−1 was assigned for the stretching vibration of O–C–O. The characteristic absorption peaks for the α- configuration of α-D-gluco-pyranose units in pullulan were detected at 916 cm−1, in addition to the two linkages of pullulan (i.e., α-(1,4) and α-(1,6)-D-glucosidic bonds) were also observable at 835 and 986 cm−1 [61]. One significant sharp band was appeared at 1424 cm−1 in IR spectra of pullulan based CQDs which was characteristic for C-C aromatic ring chain vibrations. Hence all these evidences approved the compatibility of both carrageenan and pullulan for generation of CQDs with superior decorative surface functional groups.

On the other hand, NMR spectral mapping data were presented in Fig. 5 for successive elucidation of reaction mechanism and approval for nucleation of CQDs from both of carrageenan and pullulan. Fig. 5a & b showed 1HNMR for CQDs prepared from carrageenan and pullulan, respectively. While, the spectra for both of carrageenan based CQDs (Fig. 5a) and pullulan based CQDs (Fig. 5b) were showed the existence of characteristic bands for protons of sp3 C-H at 1–2 ppm, protons attached to hydroxyl decorative groups at 3.5–4 ppm, protons attached to carbonyl groups at 4.5–6 ppm and aromatic or sp2 CH=CH protons at 8–8.5 ppm. Fig. 5c & d represented 13CNMR spectra of carrageenan based CQDs (Fig. 5c) and pullulan based CQDs (Fig. 5d) and it could be obviously observed that, the typical bands for CQDs at 30 ppm, 73 ppm, 165 ppm and 182 ppm, which are corresponding to sp3 carbons, carbons attached with hydroxyl groups, C=C aromatic or sp2 carbons and carbons of C=O groups, respectively, were detected in both cases.

Photoluminescence of CQDs was investigated to be mainly dependent on the decorative surface groups rather than their size [56]. Wu et al. approved that, photoluminescence of CQDs is characteristic to the special surface states which is composed of decorative functional groups on carbonic backbone [34]. Surface states are florescence centers constructed by the synergetic hybridization of chemical groups and carbon core [62]. Therefore, the optical properties of the as-prepared CQDs is attributed to the surface decorative functional groups inborn from carrageenan and pullulan with well overlapping of π-π* and n-π* transitions for the aromatized clustered CQDs. Additionally, surface decorative groups principally effect on the absorption location, leading to colorful CQDs and endowing their feasible colorimetric detections.

Hence, the optical properties of the as-synthesized CQDs were investigated via PL spectroscopy and the PL emission spectra after excitation at 340 nm and were plotted in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6a & b, the excited CQDs based on both of carrageenan and pullulan exhibited sharpened PL emission band at 454 nm after dialysis which is ranged in the green region, is similarly observed in case of CQDs prepared from pullulan as reported in literature [63–65]. The observed broad emission peaks in UV–visible absorption spectra hence approved the presence of mobile π electron and non-bonded electrons on the plenty of conjugated carbonic backbone and few heteroatoms. PL spectra of CQDs affirmed the morphological characters and the fluorescent ability of CQDs nucleated from both of carrageenan and pullulan. The PL data approved that the hydrothermally synthesized carbon quantum dots emits in visible region so it has the capability of strong fluorescence in visible range, which subsequently facilitate their applicability in biomedical purposes and has elaborated currently by the photoluminescence spectra of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anticancer potentiality of CQDs for apoptotic cell death
ce:section.ce:para: WST-1 cell proliferation assay could be identified as a colorimetric assay which is mainly based on the cleavage of a tetrazolium salt (WST-1) by succinate-tetrazolium reductase system that belongs to the respiratory chain of the mitochondria to produce formazan dye in viable cells. The greater the number of viable cells, i.e. metabolically active cells, the greater will be the amounts of produced formazan. Thus, by following up the addition of WST-1, for detection of the formazan level in the cells, it could be subsequently beneficial for quantifying the cell number. Therefore, the amount of the produced formazan is directly correlated to the number of metabolically active cells. In the current approach, WST-1 assay was performed for comparative studying of the anti-proliferative effect of CQDs prepared by exploitation of carrageenan versus CQDs prepared from pullulan on MDA-MB-231: breast cancer cell line. Fig. 7a represented an image for the samples examined in current approach with WST-1 assay, which obviously showed the color variation, as it is mainly correlated with CQDs concentrations, and it affirmed that, exposure of cancer cells to the synthesized CQDs for 48 h resulted in a concentration-dependent decrement in cell viability.

Higher cytotoxic effects of the prepared CQDs resulted in decrement in the viable cells, which in turn reflected in lesser amounts of produced formazan dye and subsequently, diminishing in the optical density. Therefore, monitoring the decrement of optical density was important for following up the decrement in the number of viable cells. Fig. 7b represented the relation between the optical density and the concentration of CQDs nucleated from pullulan (CQDs1) and that nucleated from carrageenan (CQDs2), and the plotted data significantly showed that, the optical density was greatly lowered via exposure to CQDs, while, higher concentrations was observably resulted in vanishing optical density values, however, gradual decrement in optical density was more significant in case of CQDs prepared from pullulan compared to that prepared from carrageenan. Fig. 7c showed the effect of concentration for CQDs prepared from carrageenan and pullulan on the number of dead cells, while, the plotted data declared that, both of CQDs1 and CQDs2 exhibited superior mortal effects on the treated breast cancer cells, while it acted in extreme reduction of the number of viable cells. By comparing between the detected data for CQDs prepared from carrageenan and that for CQDs prepared from pullulan, it could be obviously observed that, CQDs nucleated from pullulan exhibited higher anticancer potency. Fig. 7d showed relation between the concentration of CQDs prepared from the two applied biopolymers and the cell viability percent, as CQDs originated from pullulan decreased the cell proliferation and cell viability percent of MDA-MB-231 cancer cells by 98.5% to 42.1% by increasing the concentration from 10 μg/mL up to 100 μg/mL. While, in case of CQDs nucleated from carrageenan, it showed to exhibit good anti-proliferative activity, as the cell viability percentage was diminished from 67.7% to 58.7% with increment of CQDs concentration from 100 μg/mL up to 1000 μg/mL. These findings significantly affirmed that; CQDs generated from both of carrageenan and pullulan under hydrothermal conditions were potentially exhibited significant effects for cell mortality toward MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cell line, however, CQDs prepared from pullulan showed more affinity for cytotoxic activity and anti-cancer action against the as-tested breast cancer cell line rather than that synthesized from carrageenan.

For affirming the biocompatibility of the prepared CQDs for the normal cells, the optical density, the number of dead cells and the cell viability percent and the estimated results were plotted in supplementary data (Fig. S1). The plotted data approved that, the synthesized CQDs from both of carrageenan and pullulan, while, diminishing in cell viability percent for normal cells after exposure to CQDs prepared form pullulan was lesser compared to that prepared from carrageenan, as with concentration from 100 up to 1000 μg/mL, decrement in cell viability was estimated with factor of 0.617 to 9.615.

For affirming the biocompatibility of the prepared CQDs for the normal cells, the optical density, the number of dead cells and the cell viability percent and the estimated results were plotted in supplementary data (Fig. S1). The plotted data approved that, the synthesized CQDs from both of carrageenan and pullulan, while, diminishing in cell viability percent for normal cells after exposure to CQDs prepared form pullulan was lesser compared to that prepared from carrageenan, as with concentration from 100 up to 1000 μg/mL, decrement in cell viability was estimated with factor of 0.617 to 9.615.

The capability of any compound for destroying alive cells is known as cytotoxicity, while, such compounds were supposed to act in cell mortality via inducing necrosis which means accidental cell death, or apoptosis which means programmed cell death (Fig. 7e). According to literature [66], apoptosis which is identified as programmed cell-death is considered as a preferable mode of action for the antitumor drugs. Recent researches were considerably interested in investigation of potential medicines for motivating the apoptosis in the malignant cells. Activation of caspases is well-known to play an essential role in motivation and progressing of apoptosis. Caspase-3 as one of endoproteases, is an enzyme which regulates apoptosis signaling networks and inflammation, while, Caspase-3 as an executioner caspase has a vital role in coordinating the destruction of cellular structures such as degradation of cytoskeletal proteins or DNA fragmentation [67].

In agreement to literature [68], the anticancer potentiality and anti-proliferative activity of the prepared CQDs in the current approach could explained as, exposure to CQDs was mainly caused the activation of caspase-3 of MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cell lines, and subsequently resulting in apoptotic cell death, as the as-synthesized CQDs contained free mobile electrons and it could stop the growth of the as-tested breast cancer cells by generating of reactive oxygen species (ROS) to activate caspase-3, which acted directly in apoptosis.

It could summarized that, the synthesized CQDs from both of carrageenan and pullulan exhibited high water and phosphate buffer solubility, in addition to its good stability and high toxicity on cancer cells, and the prepared CQDs in the current work could be ascribed as superiorly bio-applicable antitumor candidates. Additionally, the data approved the seniority pullulan over carrageenan in nucleation of CQDs exploitable as anticancer laborer.

Numerous of infectious diseases which were caused by viruses were the dramatic cause of death worldwide. A lot of these diseases became interestingly more considerable to be treated especially if the pathogens were modified to become multidrug resistant, which unfortunately leading to higher medical costs and increment of mortality rates. Few studies were recently designed for investigating the efficiency of CQDs for viral inactivation and/or reducing the infection rates. Du et al., were reported that CQDs could significantly exhibit antiviral potency against pseudorabies virus as model for DNA virus and porcine reproductive respiratory syndrome virus as model for RNA virus via inhibiting their multiplication [69]. Type I interferons (IFNs), which includes two main types of IFN-α and IFN-β, are well-known to be the best instinctive antiviral immune compounds with a powerful antiviral performance to viral infection in human body [70]. So, it was supposed that, the inhibitory effect of CQDs was rationalized to be attributed to motivate the production of ISGs (Interferon Simulated Genes) and activating of IFN-α, which consequently acted in inhibition of viral multiplication [69]. Another report assumed that, CQDs could act as veridical reagent via induction of apoptosis, which was mediated mainly through generating of reactive oxygen species (ROS), motivating the release of cytochrome c from mitochondria, activating of caspase3, cleaving of phospholipase C-γ1, and lastly fragmenting DNA biomolecules [71] (Fig. 8c).

Therefore, investigation of the antiviral performance of CQDs could be ascribed as a highly promising new platform for antiviral laborers. In the current study, a comparative study between the antiviral performance against corona virus (MERS-CoV) of pullulan based CQDs versus carrageenan based CQDs was systematically studied. It was estimated that, pullulan based CQDs exhibited by 50% cytotoxic concentration (CC50) value of 4 mg/l, while carrageenan based CQDs showed CC50 value of 1.8 mg/l. These findings affirmed that, the cytotoxic effect of carrageenan based CQDs on viral cells was significantly higher than that of pullulan based CQDs. The anti-proliferative effects of pullulan based CQDs versus carrageenan based CQDs were further confirmed by a plaque reduction assay (Fig. 8a), and the estimated data showed higher anti-proliferative effects of carrageenan based CQDs (CQDs2) rather than pullulan based CQDs (CQDs1). Viral counting was performed and expressed in proliferation (PFU/ml×104) which was evaluated with different concentrations of CQDs colloids, while, higher estimated value of PFU means higher number of alive viral cells. The data showed that, using of 0.312 μg/L of CQDs1 and CQDs2 resulted in PFU values of 2.5 and 2.25, respectively, while using higher concentration of 2.5 μg/L resulted in significant reduction in the detected value of PFU to be1.75 & 1.25 for CQDs1 & CQDs2, respectively. The viral inhibition percent was also estimated for viral cells treated with both of pullulan and carrageenan based CQDs with different concentrations (Fig. 8b), and the results showed that 0.625 μg/L of CQDs1 and CQDs2 exhibited by viral inhibition percent of 28.6 & 35.3%. However, higher concentration up to 2.5 μg/L of CQDs1 & CQDs2 exhibited by viral inhibition percent of 44.3& 59.5%, respectively. Results of plaque reduction assay affirmed that, compared to pullulan based CQDs, carrageenan based CQDs possessed superior anti-proliferative effects against corona viral cells.

The present study aimed to produce CQDs by a facile, green, and low-cost hydrothermal technique, while, a comparative study was currently presented for the affinity and accessibility of carrageenan versus pullulan in nucleation of hydrophilic CQDs as antitumor and antiviral laborers, with analyzing their cytotoxic effects on human breast cancer cells and middle east respiratory syndrome-related coronavirus for investigation of their apoptogenic activities. The synthesized CQDs were dialyzed for production of monodispersed/highly purified CQDs with mean size of 2.1 nm for both of carrageenan based CQDs and pullulan based CQDs. Successive synthesis of CQDs was confirmed via several instrumental analyses like, UV–visible spectroscopy, zetasizer, transmission electron microscopy, Raman spectroscopy, FTIR, 1HNMR & 13CNMR spectra. The photoluminescence properties of the synthesized CQDs were investigated. The anticancer potentiality of CQDs was discovered via WST-1 cell proliferation assay for analyzing their cell mortality effects against breast cancer cells in case of carrageenan based CQDs versus pullulan based CQDs, while, pullulan showed seniority over carrageenan in nucleation of CQDs with higher anticancer activities against breast cancer cells, and the synthesized CQDs could be considered as economical and easily accessible sources for antitumor reagents to be applicable in cancer chemotherapy. In contrast, estimation of antiviral performance of the prepared CQDs confirmed the priority of carrageenan compared to pullulan in preparation of CQDs as antiviral laborer.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
Activity for the prepared CQDs against breast normal cells; [a] optical density, [b] number of died cells and [c] cell viability percentage.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2020.12.151.
Authors are equally contributed in the current work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.
